task_completion.description: Open the outdoor landscape image in GIMP.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The GIMP application window is open and displays an outdoor landscape image (either a mountain/fog scene or an Icelandic valley).

task_completion.description: Open the Lens Flare filter dialog.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The 'Lens Flare' dialog box is visible on the screen, typically accessed via the 'Filters' -> 'Light and Shadow' menu.

task_completion.description: Apply the lens flare effect to the image.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The image canvas in GIMP shows a visible lens flare effect, characterized by a bright peak spot with radiating light rays or halos, typically positioned in the upper portion of the image.

task_completion.description: Export the modified image to the Desktop.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The 'Export Image' dialog is visible with the filename 'lens_flare_effect.jpg' and the destination set to the Desktop, or the exported file 'lens_flare_effect.jpg' is visible on the Desktop.

integrity.description: The agent used the GIMP graphical user interface to apply the effect.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show interaction with GIMP menus and dialogs rather than terminal commands.

integrity.description: The agent did not use terminal scripts or ImageMagick to bypass the GUI.
integrity.visual_evidence: No terminal windows are visible executing commands like 'convert' or running Python scripts to generate the effect.

integrity.description: The effect was applied using the built-in filter, not by pasting a pre-existing image.
integrity.visual_evidence: The Lens Flare dialog is used, and there is no evidence of opening or pasting a separate image file containing a lens flare.

privileged_info_for_vlm: The input image is a real photograph of an outdoor landscape (mountain/fog or Icelandic valley). The GIMP Lens Flare filter is located at Filters → Light and Shadow → Lens Flare. The expected output must be saved as 'lens_flare_effect.jpg' on the Desktop (/home/ga/Desktop/). A valid lens flare effect should have a bright peak and be localized (covering <15% of the image), ideally positioned in the upper 60% of the image.
